data.title: Insider trades: Nike, Marvell Technology among notable names
data.description:
A weekly overview of insider trading, highlighting significant purchases and sales by investors, directors, and executives. The transactions occurred between April 6, 2026, and April 10, 2026.

  • Marvell Technology (MRVL) EVP and Chief Legal Officer Mark Casper sold 6,900 shares at $109.45 per share for $755,205, reducing his holdings by 32.57% to 14,286 shares.
  • Lululemon (LULU) Chief Brand Officer Nicole Neuburger sold 622 shares at $161 per share for $100,142, lowering her holdings by 3.14% to 19,156 shares.
  • Credo Technology (CRDO) CEO William Brennan reported a sale worth $1.2M. Brennan let go of 12,298 shares at $101.45 each, diluting his holding by 0.61% to 2M.
  • Nebius’ (NBIS) Chief Infrastructure Officer Andrey Korolenko sold 31,357 shares at $111.20 per share, generating $3.49M and cutting his stake by 5.09% to 585,209 shares.
  • Ulta Beauty (ULTA) director George Mrkonic reduced his holdings by nearly 16% to 2,383 shares. Mrkonic sold 452 for $539.29 each, for a total worth of $243,759.
  • Nike (NKE) director Robert Swan purchased 11,781 shares at $42.44 per share, totaling $500,002 and increasing his holdings to 56,654 shares.
  • Victoria’s Secret & Co. (VSCO) director Anne Sheehan acquired 4,842 shares at $45.86 per share for $222,054, bringing her total stake to 34,261 shares. Meanwhile, director Donna James bought 2,175 shares for $46.23 each, for a total value of $100,550, increasing her holdings to 63,097 shares.

data.title: Direct negotiations between U.S. and Iran start in Pakistan
data.description:
Direct negotiations between delegations from the U.S. and Iran began in the Pakistani capital of Islamabad on Saturday under a two-week ceasefire agreement between Washington and Tehran.

Citing sources in Islamabad, Al Jazeera reported that the two teams are negotiating directly in the presence of Pakistani mediators. Previously, the U.S. and Iranian teams were expected to communicate with each other from separate rooms, with Pakistani officials anticipated to pass messages between them.

Earlier in the day, Pakistan confirmed the arrival of the Iranian delegation led by the speaker of parliament, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi in Islamabad.

A few hours later, U.S. Vice President JD Vance landed in Islamabad. He was met by U.S. Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and President Donald Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner.

The meeting comes six weeks after the U.S. and Israeli military campaign in Iran began in late February, sending oil prices sharply higher amid Tehran’s near-total blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, through which about 20% of global seaborne oil trade normally takes place.

Dear readers, we recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: SA Asks: Is now a good time to buy solar stocks?
data.description:
Given the ongoing volatility in the global oil and gas markets, is now a good time to buy solar energy stocks?

Seeking Alpha analysts WideAlpha and Ragmar Rikberg weigh in. 

WideAlpha: The boring answer is “it depends.” In general, we do not like PV manufacturers, as it is a very commoditized industry. We would therefore avoid the Invesco Solar ETF (TAN), as it has a high concentration in this segment of the solar sector.

We prefer indirect exposure through advanced system components, such as Enphase Energy (ENPH) with its microinverters and batteries. 

Nextpower (NXT) is also interesting, even if utility solar trackers are somewhat commoditized. They have found ways to differentiate themselves with things like their TrueCapture software that optimizes positioning and increases yield. Nextpower (NXT) is also expanding into other areas, from foundations to automatic monitoring/cleaning, and soon to power inverters.

Another option is companies that own and develop solar and wind projects. Some that we have written about in the past have been taken private, such as Innergex and Atlantica Yield. More recently, Boralex (BRLXF) also announced it is being taken private. Pure plays remaining in the sector include Brookfield Renewable (BEP) (BEPC) and Northland Power (NPIFF). 

The solar/wind producer we find most attractive, ERG SpA (ERGSY), trades on the Milan exchange. The company has wind and solar assets in several European countries, including Italy, Germany, and the U.K. It also has a dividend yield of ~4.5%, and its valuation multiples are below its historical averages. ERG has several opportunities in the development pipeline, including wind repowering, utility solar, and battery storage projects.

Ragmar Rikberg: Since U.S. natural gas prices haven’t risen alongside global gas prices—and given that natural gas remains the main source of electricity generation in the U.S. (about 40%, followed by nuclear at 18%)—I don’t see any particular catalyst for solar stocks in the U.S. right now.

That said, after analyzing First Solar (FSLR), I still found it to be an attractive investment, regardless of the current volatility in energy prices.

What surprised me during the FSLR analysis was that, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration, renewables are projected to be the only electricity‑generation segment to grow in 2026, and that forecast was made months before the Iran crisis began.

In addition, I recently analyzed EV maker Rivian (RIVN)—not a pure solar play, but still connected to the broader renewable‑energy ecosystem. It could attract more attention as fuel prices at gas stations have spiked, and with the new R2 model coming out this spring.

  • Top Renewable Electricity Stocks 
  • Top Semiconductor Materials & Equipment Stocks
  • Top Electrical Components & Equipment Stocks

data.title: Trending stocks this week as ceasefire hopes lift sentiment amid U.S.-Israel-Iran Tensions
data.description:
Wall Street’s main indexes posted weekly gains this week as investors digested hopes of a ceasefire deal between the U.S. and Israel and Iran’s regime. For the week, the S&P gained 3.6%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite rose 4.7%, and the blue-chip Dow added 3.0%.

Applied Materials (AMAT) shares surged 8% Wednesday after the semiconductor equipment manufacturer revealed a pair of new chipmaking tools designed to operate with atomic-level precision for the new wave of AI chips. These new systems are designed to create the smallest features in the most advanced logic chips. The astronomical demand for artificial intelligence computing power has led to the introduction of gate-all-around transistors at 2nm.

Fastly (FSLY), along with its competitors Akamai (AKAM) and Cloudflare (NET), tumbled Friday after Anthropic (ANTHRO) launched its Claude Managed Agents. Fastly had plunged 22%, Akamai sunk 17%, and Cloudflare collapsed by 14%. Investors are apparently viewing Claude Managed Agents as a direct infrastructure competitor to these platforms.

Organon (OGN) closed 28% higher Friday after media reports from India indicated that Mumbai-based generic drugmaker Sun Pharmaceutical Industries had submitted a ~$12B binding bid for the company following months of due diligence, with financing support from JPMorgan Chase & Co. and MUFG.

Ares Management (ARES) declined 4% after reports that it is sounding out investors for a new ~$20B direct lending fund, even as rising redemption pressure weighs on sentiment across private credit. The firm recently capped withdrawals from its Ares Strategic Income Fund after receiving $1.2B in Q1 redemption requests, part of a broader ~$20B+ pullback from the asset class impacting peers like Apollo Global Management, Ares, Blackstone, Blue Owl Capital, and KKR, signaling growing investor caution despite continued fundraising efforts.

Medicare insurers rallied after the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services said it will raise average Medicare Advantage payments by 2.48% for 2027, amounting to more than $13B in additional funding. The move lifted insurer stocks, including Elevance Health (ELV) +3%, UnitedHealth Group (UNH) +9%, CVS Health (CVS) +7%, and Centene Corporation (CNC) +3%, as investors priced in improved reimbursement outlooks.

data.title: Abbott ordered to pay $70M in damages in Chicago baby formula case
data.description:
A jury in Chicago on Friday increased the total damages that Abbott Laboratories (ABT) must pay in a case alleging that the company’s baby formula caused a fatal bowel disease in preterm infants up to $70M.

After a months-long trial, the jurors in Illinois state court on Thursday ordered the MedTech giant to pay $53M in compensatory damages to four mothers who accused the company of hiding that its formula for premature infants can cause necrotizing enterocolitis ((NEC)).

The jurors reconvened on Friday to impose an additional $17M in punitive damages on Abbott (ABT). The company said it disagreed with the verdict and would appeal.

"Science was ignored in this case," the maker of Similac formulas said in a statement, adding, “This verdict, and the continued pursuit of a theory at odds with regulators and the medical community, risks eliminating vital options for doctors and the most vulnerable infants.”

The verdict marked the second trial loss for the Abbott Park, Illinois-based firm, which faces more than 1,700 similar lawsuits in federal and state courts that could potentially cost the company billions of dollars in additional damages.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Netflix, Carvana and Marvell Technology among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the red this week as the constant headlines on the ongoing Middle East conflict kept investors on edge.

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) and Dow (DJI) both rose over 3%. 

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Netflix earns bullish call ahead of earnings

Goldman Sachs upgraded Netflix (NFLX) to Buy from Neutral, saying with the distraction of battling Paramount Skydance for control of Warner Bros. behind it, the streaming giant can now focus on its “strategic roadmap” of capital allocation towards content, including more live events, and returning capital to shareholders.

Goldman Sachs analyst Eric Sheridan argues that Netflix should achieve double-digit revenue growth over the next three to four years thanks to its advertising business—expected to grow to ~$9.5B by 2030—and increased subscription prices.

The brokerage lifted its price target for the stock by 20% to $120.

BofA downgraded Carvana as headwinds create balanced risk/reward

BofA downgraded Carvana (CVNA) to Neutral from Buy, with analyst Michael McGovern saying that the company faces a more challenged outlook than it did heading into 2026.

“We think the company is positioned for high levels of sustained growth long term but think the medium term could see some level of increasing industry competition, with consumer demand resilient but [gross profit per unit] seeing some headwinds,” McGovern said.

UBS downgrades ServiceNow, slashes PT

UBS downgraded ServiceNow (NOW) to Neutral from Buy as it expects revenue beats for the next few quarters to be less than normal and current remaining performance obligations to decline slightly.

UBS slashed its price target on the stock to $100 from $170.

"To date, our view has been that ServiceNow is better positioned for this AI era relative to other application software firms, and hence it has been our only Buy-rated application software stock, as we've been quite cautious on the broader group," said UBS analysts Karl Keirstead and Claire Gerder in an investor note. "Given that our confidence in that view has weakened and we're hearing more anecdotes of non-AI apps software budget pressure, we're moving to a Neutral rating despite the material YTD de-rating in the stock to 15x 2026 FCF."

LyondellBasell gets downgrade from BofA

Bank of America downgraded chemical producer LyondellBasell Industries (LYB) to Underperform from Neutral, arguing that much of the recent upside driven by geopolitical disruptions is already reflected in the stock.

“Shares have rallied on Iran conflict tailwinds which we expect to be fleeting,” the analysts wrote, noting that pricing strength tied to supply disruptions could fade as markets normalize.

Despite the downgrade, BofA raised its earnings forecasts for the company. The firm increased its 2026-28 projections to reflect stronger product margins and elevated energy prices, with 2026 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization expected to climb meaningfully. The bank also lifted its price objective to $68 from $55, reflecting improved near-term earnings power.

Marvell Technology upgraded on growth in optical products

Marvell Technology (MRVL) was upgraded by Barclays to Overweight from Equal Weight citing growth in optical products.

Analysts Tom O'Malley said that Marvell is first and foremost an optical company, and with ports growing rapidly, the market growth carries the name alone. Industry checks suggest optical ports should double in 2026 and double again in 2027, the analysts added.

The firm also raised the price target on the stock to $150 from $105.

Mizuho raised the price target on Dell Technologies (DELL) to $215 from $180, saying that the company could see some upside in the near term for AI servers with a potential demand/sentiment shift in the market as Dell uses its 10x AI server teams versus peers, fortress balance sheet, and estimated $85B five-quarter pipeline. The analysts noted that higher average selling prices, or ASPs, for PCs could offset some near-term unit headwinds as they see Dell taking PC share in 2026.

BTIG downgraded Zscaler (ZS) to Neutral from Buy due in part to its share price drop and increasing competition. The brokerage also removed Zscaler from its “Top Picks” list for the first half of 2026.

data.title: Replimune plans layoffs as FDA rejects cancer drug
data.description:
Replimune (REPL) has announced that it has no other option but to reduce its workforce and scale back U.S. manufacturing operations after the FDA on Friday declined to approve its lead asset, RP1, targeted at a skin cancer known as melanoma.

Following a trading halt during the day, Replimune (REPL) crashed ~63% in the post-market after the company confirmed the FDA’s Complete Response Letter in connection with its Biologics License Application.

The BLA targeted RP1’s use for patients with advanced melanoma as a second-line therapy with nivolumab, a cancer treatment marketed by Bristol Myers (BMY) as Opdivo.

The Massachusetts-based biotech rebuked the decision, noting that it contradicted the FDA’s prior views regarding RP1 communicated during a Type A meeting last year following the treatment’s initial rejection by the regulator in July 2025.

“It is deeply disappointing that the FDA has not exercised regulatory flexibility to meet patients’ needs given the data supporting strong efficacy and the favorable safety profile,” CEO Sushil Patel remarked.

“A treatment desperately needed by patients will not be available. Not because the medicine failed. Because the system did,” he added.

data.title: Third Point gives up proxy battle at CoStar, sells stake: Reuters
data.description:
Activist hedge fund Third Point will not seek a proxy fight at CoStar Group (CSGP) and has sold its entire ownership stake in the real estate services provider, Reuters reported, citing sources familiar with the matter and a letter sent to investors.

Led by billionaire investor Dan Loeb, Third Point exited its position after noting that its plans to make CoStar (CSGP) focus on its core operations would not rescue the company, whose shares have plunged more than 50% over the past 12 months.

Shares of CoStar (CSGP) rose in January after Third Point confirmed its proxy fight, noting that the ‌owner of Apartments.com and Homes.com should focus more on its core commercial business and wind down or divest its residential operations.

"We no longer believe that our original thesis ​holds true today and have disposed of our position in its entirety," Loeb wrote in the letter to investors on Friday.

"Despite our efforts, CEO Andy Florance has continued what can only ​be seen as ⁠a reckless drain on a majority of the company’s operating income into Homes.com and related acquisitions even as the share price has continued to plummet," Loeb added.

CoStar (CSGP) counts the New York-based hedge fund among its 15 largest shareholders.

data.title: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman’s home hit with Molotov cocktail; suspect arrested
data.description:
A 20-year-old male has been arrested after allegedly throwing a Molotov cocktail at OpenAI (OPENAI) CEO Sam Altman’s home and threatening to torch the AI startup’s San Francisco headquarters, a spokesperson for the company confirmed on Friday.

According to The Wall Street Journal, the ChatGPT maker stated in a memo sent to employees that all of its San Francisco offices were open on Friday and that the police and security presence around its buildings had been increased.

The person managed to approach Altman’s residence in San Francisco’s Russian Hill neighborhood at approximately 4 a.m. local time and threw an incendiary device toward the home, the memo read. According to the San Francisco Police Department, the device caused a fire at an exterior gate, but there were no injuries.

In an X post, the San Francisco police added that about an hour later, its officers responded to an incident involving a suspect who threatened to burn down a building on the 1400 block of Third Street. OpenAI’s (OPENAI) headquarters are located at 1455 Third St., San Francisco.

After finding he was the same suspect involved in the earlier incident, officers detained the individual, police said, adding that charges are still pending and an investigation into the incident is ongoing.

“Early this morning, someone threw a Molotov cocktail at Sam Altman’s home and also made threats at our San Francisco headquarters,” the spokesperson for OpenAI (OPENAI) said, adding, "Thankfully, no one was hurt."

Altman addressed the attack in a post on his personal blog on Friday, noting that public criticism targeting the AI industry stems from “sincere concern about the incredibly high stakes of this technology.”

“While we have that debate, we should de-escalate the rhetoric and tactics and try to have fewer explosions in fewer homes, figuratively and literally,” he added.

data.title: Pump prices likely to stay high after posting biggest monthly increase in nearly 60 years
data.description:
Inflation in the U.S. soared last month, the Labor Department reported Friday, with gasoline prices posting their largest monthly increase in nearly 60 years due to the Middle East war that has pushed average retail prices above $4/gal for the first time in more than ​three years.

The Consumer Price Index jumped 0.9% in March, the Bureau of Labor Statistics said, highlighted by a 21.2% month-to-month surge in gasoline prices, the largest since the government started consistently tracking the data in 1967; other motor fuels, which include diesel, also soared by a monthly record 30.8%.

While the U.S.-Iran ceasefire agreed this week might offer some short-term relief for consumers, analysts are warning that prices will continue to rise.

"There's so much uncertainty still around what this ceasefire means, and when and how fuel starts to flow through the Strait of Hormuz again, retailers are not going to drop prices sharply in the face of those unknowns," Shon Hiatt, director of the Zage Business of Energy Initiative at the USC Marshall School of Business, told Reuters.

Retail fuel prices typically rise much faster than they fall, as fuel sellers will work through higher-priced inventory and seek a higher degree of ​certainty of future supply to avoid losses, Hiatt said.

Regardless of whether the ceasefire holds, insurance costs will remain higher than before the war, and ships will ​be hesitant to cross the Strait of Hormuz, StoneX energy strategist Alex Hodes said in a note.

"Markets still will be elevated throughout the rest of the year with an elevated geopolitical risk premium," Hodes wrote, adding that diesel and jet fuel especially will remain on edge, as supply has been ​tighter than other refined products.

"The combination of market shocks, the potential for prolonged disruption, and shifting expectations creates the conditions for higher prices to spread, especially as initial energy cost increases ripple through goods and services across the economy," Competitive Enterprise Institute analyst Steve Swedberg said.

Crude futures fell in relatively light trading Friday ahead of the start of talks this weekend between the U.S. and Iran that are considered crucial to the success of the ceasefire and reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

"Traders have evacuated the market for the most part, with $7 moves like yesterday seemingly happening with little participation from human traders other than what they need to do in terms of hedging or cleaning up positions to get even flatter," TP ICAP's Scott Shelton said in a note. "Maybe after this weekend we'll get some clarity on whether or not Iran and the U.S. are so far apart that they can’t come to an agreement."

This week, front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery fell 13.4% to $96.57/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery tumbled 12.7% to $95.20/bbl, the largest one-week percentage decline for both benchmarks since April 2020; on Friday, U.S. crude closed down 1.3% and Brent slipped 0.7%.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) ended the week lower as the market shifts into the low-demand spring shoulder season; the front-month Nymex May contract settled down 0.8% Friday at $2.648/MMBtu for a 5.4% weekly loss.

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), fell 3.9% for the week.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)
